ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for flavoured milk (less sugar); SNOMED CT coverage for processed dairy beverages is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-serve carton of flavoured milk (less sugar) is approximately 200 ml, equivalent to about 207 g, commonly consumed as a snack or school beverage in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Flavoured Milk (Less Sugar) — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Flavoured Milk (Less Sugar), food code F01030004. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from this record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 34 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for flavoured milk (chocolate milk, reduced fat), which is the closest approved analogous product. Flavoured milk with added sugar and dairy protein matrix consistently yields GI in the 24–40 range. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (34 × 17.3) / 100 ≈ 5.9, using a 207 g serving with 8.37 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar present; fibre <1 g/100 g; sugar-sweetened beverage classification
hypertension.guidance: Despite low sodium (64 mg/100 g), the presence of added sugar (5.6 g/100 g) in a liquid form independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance; individuals managing hypertension should choose plain unsweetened low-fat milk to obtain the same calcium and potassium benefits without the added sugar load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) with added sugar; liquid glucose absorption is rapid
type2_diabetes.guidance: This product is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage because sugar is added during manufacture; even 'less sugar' variants are not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, as liquid-form added sugars raise postprandial blood glucose rapidly and should be avoided in favour of plain unsweetened milk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar in liquid form; low saturated fat but SSB classification carries independent CVD risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages, including reduced-sugar variants with added sugar, is associated with elevated triglycerides and increased cardiovascular risk; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit this product and prefer plain low-fat milk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The added sugar content (5.6 g/100 g) may promote systemic inflammation relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; plain unsweetened low-fat milk is preferred to obtain the bone-protective calcium (163 mg/100 g) without the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Flavoured Milk (Less Sugar) is a processed dairy beverage made from pasteurised cow's milk with added flavouring agents and a reduced quantity of sugar compared to standard flavoured milk products. It is a liquid drink with a smooth, mildly sweet taste and the characteristic creamy texture of dairy milk. In Singapore, it is widely available in single-serve cartons of approximately 200 ml and is commonly consumed as a school snack, post-exercise drink, or light refreshment. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 62 kcal of energy, 4.4 g of protein, 0.98 g of fat, and 8.37 g of carbohydrate including 5.6 g of sugar. It is a meaningful source of calcium (163 mg/100 g), supporting bone health. Despite the 'less sugar' label, added sugar is confirmed present during manufacture, which classifies this product as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) for clinical purposes. This SSB classification makes it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension, as liquid-form added sugars raise postprandial blood glucose rapidly and are independently associated with elevated blood pressure and cardiovascular risk. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 34 (low GI), reflecting the moderating effect of the dairy protein and fat matrix on glucose absorption, but the SSB classification overrides GI-based suitability for diabetic dietary guidance. Sodium is low at 64 mg/100 g. Iron is reported as 0.0 mg/100 g by the HPB source, confirmed as a negligible trace amount in this product formulation.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (food code F01030004) with a confidence of 0.95 in the input record. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for a closely matched flavoured milk product.
notes: iron_mg is reported as 0.0 by the HPB source (food code F01030004); this is a confirmed trace/negligible value for this dairy beverage formulation — confirmed zero per Singapore HPB Food Composition Database. saturated_fat_g of 0.64 is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for reduced-fat flavoured milk (NDB 01211) as the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat; this is documented as an adapted value. potassium_mg of 180.0 is estimated from USDA FoodData Central for low-fat flavoured milk as the HPB record does not report potassium separately. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this specific product as of 2026-04-16. This food is a generic processed dairy beverage; however, as it is consumed in Singapore, multilingual names and aliases for Singapore's official languages (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) have been added to meet G6 compliance.
